question.text: Under Section 2(h) of the Indian Succession Act 1925, a "will" is defined as a legal declaration. In the context of a will, who is the person responsible for administering the estate as per the testator's wishes?

answer.text: Testator — the person who makes the will
Legatee — the person who receives the bequest
Executor — the person appointed to administer the estate
Probate officer — the court-appointed official who validates the will

explanation.text: **The Logic:** In a will, there are three key parties: Testator (maker of the will), Legatee (receiver of the bequest), and Executor (administrator of the estate). The Executor ensures the testator's wishes are carried out. The will is effective only after death of the testator. **The Trap:** Candidates may confuse "Executor" with "Probate officer." Probate is a court process to validate the will, but the Executor is named in the will itself by the testator. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM X-B tests will-related terminology. Section 2(h) defines "will" while Section 59 covers testamentary capacity. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always advise clients to name a competent and willing Executor in their will. A responsible family member or a trusted professional is ideal — the Executor's role is administrative, not beneficial.
